summary:
Ivan III of Moscow ('Ivan the Great,' reigned 1462–1505 AD) was the ruler who transformed the Grand Duchy of Moscow from one of several competing Russian principalities under [[event:2305|Golden Horde]] suzerainty into an independent, unified Russian state that became the foundation of the Russian Empire. Through a combination of military conquest, diplomatic marriage, and patient political maneuvering, Ivan tripled Moscow's territory by absorbing Novgorod (1478), Tver (1485), and other principalities, and in 1480 ended Russian tribute-payment to the Golden Horde by standing down a Horde army at the Ugra River without battle — the 'Great Stand on the Ugra' that effectively ended the Tatar Yoke.

Ivan's marriage to Sophia Palaiologina — niece of the last Byzantine emperor [[event:2240|Constantine XI]] — in 1472 gave Moscow a claim to the Byzantine imperial heritage, and Ivan adopted the Byzantine double-headed eagle as Moscow's symbol, began calling himself 'Tsar' (Caesar), and proclaimed Moscow the 'Third Rome' — the continuation of Rome and Constantinople as the center of Orthodox Christian civilization. He hired Italian architects to rebuild the Moscow Kremlin, producing the Cathedral of the Assumption and Cathedral of the Archangel that still define the Kremlin's architectural character. Ivan created Russia's first comprehensive legal code (Sudebnik, 1497) and established diplomatic relations with Western European courts, beginning Russia's self-conscious positioning as a major European power. His policies laid the institutional and ideological foundations for the absolutist Russian monarchy that his grandson Ivan IV ('the Terrible') would develop in much more extreme form.
